name: ema
whatItMeasures: The EMA gives more weight to recent prices, making it more responsive to new information than the SMA. The 12-day and 26-day EMAs are the standard pair used in trend-following strategies and form the basis of the MACD indicator. A bullish crossover occurs when the 12-day EMA crosses above the 26-day EMA, and a bearish crossover when it crosses below.

parameters.name: Short Period
parameters.rationale: The 12-day EMA represents roughly two trading weeks and captures short-term momentum shifts. It is the standard short EMA used in the MACD calculation.

parameters.name: Long Period
parameters.rationale: The 26-day EMA represents roughly one trading month and provides a baseline for intermediate-term trend direction. It is the standard long EMA used in the MACD calculation.

howToRead: When the 12-day EMA is above the 26-day EMA, the short-term trend is bullish.
When the 12-day EMA crosses below the 26-day EMA, momentum is shifting bearish.
Price consistently above both EMAs suggests a strong uptrend.
The gap between the two EMAs indicates the strength of the current momentum — a widening gap means accelerating trend.
EMA crossovers react faster than SMA crossovers, giving earlier signals but also more false signals in choppy markets.

signalRules.neutral: EMAs are converging or intertwined with no clear directional crossover.

limitations: Faster response to price changes also means more false signals in sideways markets.
EMA crossovers are still lagging indicators — they confirm rather than predict.
In volatile markets, frequent crossovers can lead to whipsaw losses.
The exponential weighting can overreact to sudden price spikes that quickly reverse.
evidence: In a 100-year backtest of the DJIA conducted by Liberatedstocktrader.com, the EMA 12/26 crossover strategy was identified as the highest-performing moving average configuration. Its responsiveness to recent price action provided an edge over the standard SMA in capturing trend changes earlier, resulting in superior risk-adjusted returns over the full century of data.
